title: GPS Jamming Sparks Ghost Ships and Chaos in the Middle East
description: Widespread GPS jamming in the Middle East is creating hazardous maritime conditions as commercial vessels appear miles inland.
lede: A relentless wave of GPS jamming is scrambling ship coordinates in the Strait of Hormuz, rendering critical navigation systems dangerously inaccurate. Dozens of massive commercial vessels are being tracked hovering over dry land, raising the imminent threat of catastrophic maritime collisions in one of the world's busiest oil transit routes.

sections.heading: What Happened
paragraphs: In waters off the coasts of Iran, the United Arab Emirates, and Qatar, marine intelligence analysts have identified at least 35 distinct clusters of ships broadcasting falsified locations. Vessel Automatic Identification Systems (AIS), which broadcast coordinates to prevent collisions, are being systematically disrupted by intense electromagnetic interference.

The spoofing causes ships to bunch together in impossibly tight, perfect circles or register coordinates far inland. With the airspace over Iran currently closed, researchers are relying on satellite radar data to track the invisible electronic warfare spilling across the region.

The chaos echoes similar interference documented during last year's 12-day conflict between Israel and Iran, but analysts warn the current scale is unprecedented. Neither side has officially claimed responsibility for the interference.

sections.heading: Why It Matters
paragraphs: The Strait of Hormuz is a vital chokepoint for global oil distribution. Massive 300-meter-long tankers carrying hundreds of thousands of tonnes of crude rely on accurate AIS data to navigate safely, especially at night or in low visibility. Without reliable tracking, these lumbering vessels risk devastating collisions.

Military analysts strongly suspect Iran is responsible for the localized jamming, utilizing domestically produced technology or equipment sourced from Russia and China. Meanwhile, U.S. forces in the region are reportedly deploying their own jamming systems to shield bases and assets from drone strikes and GNSS-guided weapons.

sections.heading: The Bigger Picture
paragraphs: This invisible battle highlights a severe vulnerability in modern commercial infrastructure. Standard Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) signals are notoriously weak and easy to overpower. While the military relies on heavily encrypted "M-Code" GPS to resist interference, civilian and commercial operations are left exposed.

The disruption stretches beyond the Middle East, with similar GPS spoofing reported daily in the conflict in Ukraine and heavily trafficked European corridors like the Baltic Sea. It underscores how electronic warfare is increasingly becoming a standard tactical tool with sweeping global consequences.

sections.heading: What's Next
paragraphs: The demand for alternative navigation technologies is surging as commercial fleets scramble to protect their assets. Defense contractors and tech firms are rolling out anti-jamming antennas and localized positioning systems that use gyroscopes, accelerometers, or even optical star-mapping.

Despite the availability of these expensive workarounds, the global maritime industry remains structurally dependent on vulnerable open GPS signals. Experts predict a forced shift toward secure, authenticated navigation systems, warning that the current open architecture is a catastrophic accident waiting to happen.
